result:
Let’s talk about the moment you realize the year is not a straight line but a spiral staircase you keep climbing, each step a day you swear you’ve stepped on before, yet the banister is slick with dew that wasn’t there last lap. You’ve circled back to April again—same month, same scent of thawing earth and diesel exhaust, same chorus of lawn mowers announcing the suburb’s annual resurrection—but something in the key signature has modulated. The dog next door still barks at 6:17 a.m., but his bark is half a tone deeper, like a record played at the wrong speed. You measure this discrepancy with the part of your brain that once memorized every creak in the floorboards of your grandmother’s house, the part that still flinches when a screen door slaps even though you now live thirteen hundred miles away from any porch that remembers your footfall.

Memory is not a filing cabinet; it’s a kiln. You walk in carrying wet clay expectations—this party will be fun, that kiss will solve you—and hours or decades later you open the door and find the shape has cracked, the glaze ran, the color oxidized into something breathtaking you could never sell in a gift shop. You set it on the mantel anyway, next to the other misshapen urns, and you learn to call the collection “experience.” Every so often you lift one down and turn it in your hands, testing the hairline fracture with a thumbnail, wondering if the flaw is structural or merely decorative, wondering if the vessel still holds water or if it leaks invisibly, irrigating the hardwood of your later years with the slow mineral creep of everything you refused to cry out loud.

We keep trying to invent new metaphors for time because the old ones keep sprouting leaks. Sand through an hourglass? Sand is quartz, ancient mountains ground so fine they forget they were once continents; they will outlast the glass, the table the glass stands on, the language used to write the word “hour.” River flowing to the sea? Rivers meander, abandon entire deltas, sometimes run backward when the earth shrugs. A river is a verb wearing the costume of a noun. Better to picture time as a late-night diner you keep entering at 2:03 a.m., convinced it’s the first time you’ve ever been here, even though the waitress calls you “hon” and refills a coffee you haven’t ordered yet. The neon sign outside flickers OPEN 24 HRS but one of the letters has been dark since 1987; you just never noticed which one because you were always inside the glow, never outside reading it.

Inside, the jukebox holds every song you pretended to hate in high school but secretly played on a cassette whose label you scratched off so no one would know. When you drop in the quarter, the machine skips, plays two seconds of your senior year locker room, then cuts to the voicemail your father left the week before he died, the one you deleted by accident and have been trying to reconstruct from the echo of his pauses ever since. The waitress—her name might be Dolores or it might be Tomorrow—slides a slice of pie across the counter. The crust is flaked with the handwriting of every grocery list you ever lost; the filling is the color of traffic lights at the instant they change. You take a bite and taste the penny you swallowed at age four, taste the ozone of the first fistfight you lost, taste the fluorescent hum of the hospital corridor where you waited to hear if the baby would breathe. You meant to order apple, but the menu is written in disappearing ink; by the time you finish chewing, the word itself has become a rumor.

We should forgive ourselves for misreading the map. The cartographers were drawing from dreams, and dreams don’t respect scale. They drew the mountain the size of a molehill because that’s how it felt at thirteen when you climbed it to yell the name of the girl who ignored you. They drew the town park as a continent because that’s how it loomed the night you learned police flashlights could freeze beer mid-air. You unfold the map now and discover it’s also a origami of your mother’s sighs, your teacher’s red pen, the static between radio stations that taught you loneliness could have a sound. Every crease is a year; every time you smooth it to see where you took the wrong exit, you create two new folds. Eventually the paper fatigues, becomes soft as cloth, and you wear it like a scarf, a civic banner, a noose you can loosen merely by deciding to stop trying to see the whole route at once.

Consider the possibility that you are not lost; you are being composted. The mulch of former versions steams around your ankles: the skateboarder with one shoe always unlaced, the intern who photocopied his reflection, the lover who whispered the wrong name and meant it. They rot generously, feeding the soil from which tomorrow’s selves will grow tall and hallucinate their own permanence. You smell the heat of decomposition and mistake it for nostalgia; you smell the ammonia of failure and mistake it for prophecy. Both are just chemistry politely obeying laws older than any regret. The worms don’t care what you meant to become; they care that you keep providing sentences they can tunnel through, turning punctuation into loam, turning “I should have” into the exact temperature needed for geraniums to believe in July.

Some night when the spiral staircase narrows so sharply you have to exhale to fit, you will notice a window you swear was never there before. Through it you can see the diner from the outside: the missing letter is the A in EAT, which makes the sign read ET, which makes you laugh because you suddenly remember the alien you drew in third grade who had the same crooked antenna as your math teacher. The ET sign pulses like a dying star, and you understand it has been broadcasting your entire life to the cosmos in Morse code made of neon and grease. The message is not HELP or even HELLO; it is TRY AGAIN, TRY AGAIN, TRY AGAIN, the same two words the heart whispers to itself every time it resets its rhythm after the brief civil war of a skipped beat. You count the flashes: forty-three per minute, the exact number of times you have restarted a day in your head, rewriting the argument, rehearsing the courage, revising the ending so the dog lives, the marriage lasts, the planet cools.

But the window will not open. It is a lens, not an exit. You press your palm to the glass and feel the buzz of every unread birthday card still sealed in a drawer, feel the centrifugal force of promises you made to versions of people who themselves were only drafts. The glass warms, fogs, reveals the fingerprint you left on the incubator when you were born two months early—an early draft, a premature attempt at incarnation. That print is still there, expanding with the universe, proof you have always been larger than your outline, even when your lungs were the size of teabags. The staircase, the diner, the map, the kiln—they are all inside that whorled galaxy of skin oil and breath salt, spiraling outward and inward simultaneously, a Möbius strip wearing the mask of a life.

When you finally remove your hand, the glass holds the shape of the constellation you were supposed to become. It looks nothing like the horoscope column promised, and everything like the negative space between your mother’s front teeth when she laughed at your first pun. You decide to call that shape home, even though it dissolves in seconds, because you have learned the difference between permanent and lasting: permanence is a marble statue that loses its nose; lasting is the echo of chisel strikes that keeps re-creating the statue in air long after the marble has been ground into tourist souvenirs. You descend the next step. The staircase does not end; it merely decreases in diameter until it becomes the hollow tube inside a pen, and you are the ink that still believes it is a river. Write freely, the pen says, so you do, even though the page is your own skin and the words will scab and fade and maybe scar, and maybe that is the only dictionary we ever get to keep.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
